elements.sent-2411-001: Sequential learning in a multi-agent resource constrained matching market has received significant interest in the past few years.
elements.sent-2411-002: We study decentralized learning in two-sided matching markets where the demand side (aka players or agents) competes for a `large' supply side (aka arms) with potentially time-varying preferences, to obtain a stable match.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite a long line of work in the recent past, existing learning algorithms such as Explore-Then-Commit or Upper-Confidence-Bound remain inefficient for this problem.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, the per-agent regret achieved by these algorithms scales linearly with the number of arms, $K$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Motivated by the linear contextual bandit framework, we assume that for each agent an arm-mean can be represented by a linear function of a known feature vector and an unknown (agent-specific) parameter.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, our setup captures the essence of a dynamic (non-stationary) matching market where the preferences over arms change over time.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our proposed algorithms achieve instance-dependent logarithmic regret, scaling independently of the number of arms, $K$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Matching markets have become increasingly relevant in a variety of modern applications, including but not limited to school admissions, organ transplantation, and job matching.
elements.sent-2411-009: Traditionally, these markets were studied under the assumption that the demand side agents (aka players or agents) and the supply side agents (aka arms) have fixed, known preferences, allowing for stable matching via the deferred acceptance algorithm like the Gale-Shapley algorithm introduced in \cite{gale1962college .}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, in applications like crowdsourcing, online labor markets, and finance, the preferences are not given to the agents, and they must learn it over time by interacting with the environment.
elements.sent-2411-011: Modeling the matching market as multi-agent, multi-armed competitive bandits, there has been extensive work on various aspects, including coordinated centralized matching, decentralized matching, and game-theoretic analysis (see \cite{liu_centralized_2020,basu_2021,ucbd3,srikanth_uiuc ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we study a large and dynamic matching market where the number of arms $K$ is large and often exceeds the number of agents $N(\ll K)$.
elements.sent-2411-013: Further, the preferences of agents (over the arms) are not constant and change with time.
elements.sent-2411-014: Simple examples of such markets include college admission (where the number of students is much larger than the number of collages), large labor markets (where the number of tasks is much larger than the number of workers), online advertising and recommendation systems (where the number of products is much larger than the customers).
elements.sent-2411-015: This resource deficit underscores the necessity for structured exploration strategies.
elements.sent-2411-016: To alleviate the curse of a large number of arms in a standard multi-armed bandit, it is typically assumed that the arm-mean is characterized by an inner product of a known feature vector with an unknown parameter to be learned over time.
elements.sent-2411-017: This framework is popularly known as linear contextual bandits (see \cite{chu11a, OFUL ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: We adopt this paradigm in the market setup.
elements.sent-2411-019: In particular, when an agent $i$ is matched with an arm $j$ at time $t$, she receives an expected reward of $\langle \boldsymbol{x _{ij (t),\thetai\rangle$ where $\thetai$ is the agent's parameter vector and $\boldsymbol{x _{ij (t) \in \mathbb{R ^d$ is the feature of arm $j$ as observed by agent $i$ at time $t$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: With this assumption, we propose algorithms for contextual matching markets and obtain a regret independent of the number of arms.
elements.sent-2411-021: Dynamic markets, where the preferences of agents (over arms) change over time, are a common feature in many applications, such as crowd-sourcing and ride-hailing, and exogenous variables, such as holidays or special events, affect the matching market.
elements.sent-2411-022: A few recent works attempt to capture this temporal behavior (see \cite{avishek_nonstationary,maheshwari_2022 ) albeit in a slowly varying market.}
elements.sent-2411-023: We emphasize this non-stationarity of the market is reflected in our setup as the observed feature vector between any agent and arm varies with time.
elements.sent-2411-024: More precisely, we model temporal non-stationarity using a latent variable that takes a finite set of values.
elements.sent-2411-025: At each time, the realization of this latent variable governs the arm-means and preferences of all agents and arms.
elements.sent-2411-026: We emphasize that this non-stationary contextual matching market is quite involved since the complexity lies in handling scenarios where multiple agents select the same arm (collision).
elements.sent-2411-027: In such cases, the arm matches with the most preferred agent of her choice and receives a (non-zero) mean reward, while other agents receive zero rewards.
elements.sent-2411-028: The preference learning in multi-agent setup requires careful handling of the confidence set of the unknown parameters, and the difficulty exacerbates with competition.
elements.sent-2411-029: We introduce the notion of an environment where the stable matching remains fixed.
elements.sent-2411-030: An environment, namely $e$ is defined as a set of agent-side and arm-side rankings, i.e. $e = \{(\sigma^{A _{i : i \in [N]), (\sigma^{a _{j : j \in [K])\}$, where the ranking of argent $i$ and arm $j$, respectively, are denoted by $\sigma^{A _{i, e}$ and $\sigma^{a _{j, e}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Note that a (player optimal) stable matching is uniquely determined by these rankings.
elements.sent-2411-032: In this paper, we tackle two key problems.
elements.sent-2411-033: Contextual matching market (fixed environment): In the first problem, we propose the framework of linear contextual means on arms (as explained above) and address the setting where the preference ranking of agents (over arms) remains fixed throughout the duration of learning.
elements.sent-2411-034: Hence, we dub this as a fixed environment setup.
elements.sent-2411-035: Here, agents commit to a pre-defined round-robin exploration of the arms, gradually learning their preferences.
elements.sent-2411-036: The exploration proceeds in exponentially increasing sub-phases, after each of which agents communicate to verify whether they have accurately identified their preference rankings for the arms.
elements.sent-2411-037: This limited communication structure aligns with models used in \cite{tejas_2024,kong2023playeroptimalstableregretbandit .}
elements.sent-2411-038: An additional exploration subphase is triggered if any agent has not yet determined their preferences.
elements.sent-2411-039: Once preferences are correctly learned, the agents apply the Gale-Shapley algorithm to find a stable matching, which is subsequently exploited.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our algorithmic analysis deviates from prior work, where concentration bounds are typically established on the true latent preference vectors, as in \cite{varsha_dani_kakade_2008,OFUL .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Instead, we derive confidence intervals for the true mean rewards by leveraging least squares estimates to approximate the latent preference vectors and applying concentration inequalities to bound these estimated rewards.
elements.sent-2411-042: To ensure the confidence intervals shrink over time, we assume a structural condition such that the minimum eigenvalue of the outer product matrix of feature vectors increases with the number of exploration samples.
elements.sent-2411-043: This assumption, formalized in Assumption \ref{a:min-eigen-value , ensures the desired scaling of the minimum eigenvalue, a common requirement in regression problems \cite{suvrit_sra_eigenvalue .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The assumption can be realized by using standard tools from random matrix theory \cite{vershynin,Wainwright .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Our proposed algorithm achieves logarithmic per-agent regret, with the regret inversely proportional to the minimum gap between the top $N$ arms across all agents.
elements.sent-2411-046: We propose a novel reward gap condition to ensure that the regret does not scale with the uniform minimum gap over time--rather a problem adaptive gap defined in Section~\ref{subsec:regret_ana .}
elements.sent-2411-047: This is consistent with the lower bounds established in \cite{ucbd3 , which shows similar dependence is unavoidable.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Importantly, our regret is independent of the total number of arms $K$ and instead depends on the dimension $d$ of the feature vectors.
elements.sent-2411-049: This suggests that substantial savings can be realized by carefully designing feature vectors, particularly in settings where $K$ is large, as is often the case in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-050: Multi-environment contextual matching market: Here, we allow for multiple environments.
elements.sent-2411-051: We assume that each agent faces $E$ distinct rankings, corresponding to $E$ different environments, which they must learn.
elements.sent-2411-052: Section \ref{justify_env motivates the need for distinct rankings.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Intuitively, if an agent has the same ranking in two different environments, the stable matches would differ, and it may not be possible for the agent to learn the correct stable matching.
elements.sent-2411-054: Each agent has the same latent preference vector for all $E$ environments, but at each time step, one environment is presented with its corresponding set of feature vectors for the arms.
elements.sent-2411-055: The agent observes these feature vectors but does not directly observe the environments.
elements.sent-2411-056: We assume for a large block of time of size $\Omega(\log(T))$; each environment occurs a constant fraction of time (Assumption \ref{a:env ).}
elements.sent-2411-057: At each time step, environments appear arbitrarily with their associated feature sets for the arms, and agents can observe the feature vectors for all the arms.
elements.sent-2411-058: In our proposed algorithm, agents first perform round-robin exploration of the arms.
elements.sent-2411-059: We impose a similar reward gap condition on the mean rewards of the top $N$ arms.
elements.sent-2411-060: Agents can exploit features when the gaps exceed the minimum condition and learn the ranking using the confidence widths obtained thus far.
elements.sent-2411-061: Once agents have learned $E$ distinct rankings, they must identify which environment is currently active and apply the Gale-Shapley algorithm to obtain a stable matching and exploit it.
elements.sent-2411-062: However, if some agents are unable to determine the current environment, all agents restart exploration in the next phase to gather more samples, reducing the confidence width around the estimated mean rewards and aiding in the identification of the environment.

elements.sent-2411-064: While the confidence widths are not small enough to distinguish between arms with minimum gaps, agents can still correctly identify the current environment and act accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-065: To achieve this, agents leverage the rankings learned so far.
elements.sent-2411-066: Specifically, an agent forms a partial ranking of the arms, which can be distinguished based on the current confidence widths, and computes the number of inversion pairs with previously learned rankings.
elements.sent-2411-067: An {inversion pair occurs when the relative ranking of two arms is reversed between an estimated ranking and the true ranking.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Specifically, for two arms $i$ and $j$, if $i$ is ranked higher than $j$ in the true ranking but lower in the estimated ranking, or vice versa, they form an inversion pair.
elements.sent-2411-069: If there is exactly one ranking with zero inversion pairs, it corresponds to the ranking for the current environment.
elements.sent-2411-070: If multiple rankings yield zero inversion pairs, further exploration is triggered.
elements.sent-2411-071: Hence in the multiple environment setup, we need to (i) estimate (recover) ranking from round-robin exploration and (ii) perform rank matching to estimate the environment where we are in simultaneously.

elements.sent-2411-269: In this paper, we take a first step to analyze contextual matching markets which simultaneously addresses the problem of non-stationarity and scale.

elements.sent-2411-314: Suppose agent \(i\) is matched to arm \(j\).
elements.sent-2411-315: Since agent \(i\) proposed to arm \(j\) only after being rejected by all its higher-ranked preferences, agent \(i\) will not propose to any other arm unless arm \(j\) unmatches it.
elements.sent-2411-316: However, arm \(j\) will always remain matched, potentially to a different agent it prefers more.
elements.sent-2411-317: This ensures that every arm stays matched as soon as it receives a proposal.
elements.sent-2411-318: Now, to show that no agent can be rejected by all of its top \(N\) preferences, assume, for the sake of contradiction, that agent one is rejected by its top \(N\) preferred arms.
elements.sent-2411-319: This would imply that all \(N\) arms have been matched with agents they prefer over agent 1.
elements.sent-2411-320: However, since there are only \(N - 1\) other agents, it is impossible for all \(N\) arms to prefer another agent over agent 1.
elements.sent-2411-321: Therefore, agent one must be accepted by at least one of its top \(N\) preferences, which contradicts the assumption.
elements.sent-2411-322: Thus, each agent can be rejected at most \(N - 1\) times, leading to at most \(N - 1\) rejections per agent.
elements.sent-2411-323: In the worst case, each agent makes \(N - 1\) proposals before finding a match, meaning the total number of proposals is bounded by \((N - 1) \times (N - 1) + N = N^2 - N + 1\).
elements.sent-2411-324: With \(N\) proposals made in the first round, and at most one proposal per agent in each subsequent round, the total number of steps is upper bounded by \(N^2 - N + 1 - (N - 1) = N^2 - 2N + 2\).

elements.sent-2411-395: We study decentralized learning in two-sided matching markets where the demand side (aka players or agents) competes for the supply side (aka arms) with potentially time-varying preferences to obtain a stable match.
elements.sent-2411-396: Motivated by the linear contextual bandit framework, we assume that for each agent, an arm-mean may be represented by a linear function of a known feature vector and an unknown (agent-specific) parameter.
elements.sent-2411-397: Moreover, the preferences over arms depend on a latent environment in each round, where the latent environment varies across rounds in a non-stationary manner.
elements.sent-2411-398: We propose learning algorithms to identify the latent environment and obtain stable matchings simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-399: Our proposed algorithms achieve instance-dependent logarithmic regret, scaling independently of the number of arms, and hence applicable for a large market.
elements.sent-2411-400: Matching markets have become increasingly relevant in various modern applications, including school admissions, organ transplantation, and job matching.
elements.sent-2411-401: Traditionally, these markets were studied assuming that demand-side agents (also referred to as players or agents) and supply-side agents (also referred to as arms) have fixed, known preference rankings over one another.
elements.sent-2411-402: The primary goal in such settings is to achieve a stable matching, where no agent-arm pair has the incentive to deviate from their assigned match.
elements.sent-2411-403: Stability in this context corresponds to a Nash equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-404: The seminal work of Gale and Shapley~\cite{gale1962college introduced an algorithm where agents, through repeated proposals and deferred acceptance, converge to a stable matching.}
elements.sent-2411-405: However, in many real-world applications, such as crowdsourcing, online labor markets, and gig economy platforms, agents do not know their preferences a priori.
elements.sent-2411-406: Instead, they must learn these preferences through repeated interactions with the environment.
elements.sent-2411-407: In such cases, it is typically assumed that arms already know their preferences over agents, which leads to a setting known as one-sided learning.
elements.sent-2411-408: Recent research has focused extensively on this setup.
elements.sent-2411-409: Studies such as~\cite{liu_centralized_2020, liu_decentralized_2021, ucbd3, basu_2021, kong2023playeroptimalstableregretbandit have developed learning algorithms that integrate Gale-Shapley dynamics with bandit-feedback-based techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-410: This paper focuses on a matching market with $N$ agents and $K$ arms.
elements.sent-2411-411: Markets of this nature are commonly observed in real-world platforms such as Amazon Mechanical Turk, TaskRabbit, UpWork, and Jobble.
elements.sent-2411-412: In these platforms, workers are modeled as agents, and different types of work (e.g., pairwise inference, crowdsourcing, rating movies, or restaurants in Amazon Mechanical Turk) are modeled as arms.
elements.sent-2411-413: In these examples, typically external factors like the geographical location, work experience, work timings (weekday vs weekend), time of the week or month, seasonal activities (holidays), company demands, and personal information influence the agent's preference.
elements.sent-2411-414: One canonical way to model these external factors is through a feature (or context) vector.
elements.sent-2411-415: To address this, we adopt the framework of linear contextual bandits \cite{chu11a, OFUL , where rewards are modeled as a linear function.}
elements.sent-2411-416: We assume a linear structure because of its simplicity and theoretical tractability.
elements.sent-2411-417: Specifically, the expected reward for an agent matching with an arm is expressed as the inner product of an agent-specific latent reward vector and an agent-arm specific feature vector, which is revealed to the agent.
elements.sent-2411-418: Agents must learn their latent vectors to rank arms effectively based on their feature vectors, thereby eliminating the need for extensive exploration of all arms.
elements.sent-2411-419: In this work, we allow the feature vectors to vary with time, making it a non-stationary learning problem.
elements.sent-2411-420: In standard linear bandits~\cite{OFUL , changes in feature vectors do not pose significant challenges, as an agent can play the optimal arm once its latent vector is learned.}
elements.sent-2411-421: However, for multiple agents, each must play their stable matched arms.
elements.sent-2411-422: If preferences change due to arbitrarily varying feature vectors, our decentralized system would require $N^2$ rounds to relearn a stable matching using the Gale-Shapley algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-423: Therefore, allowing feature vectors to change arbitrarily is not viable in our setup, as it may lead to linear regret.
elements.sent-2411-424: To address this, we introduce additional structure to the problem by defining the notion of an environment, as detailed in Subsection~\ref{ssec:env-regret .}
elements.sent-2411-425: An environment is (uniquely) characterized by a preference ranking of agents (as well as arms).
elements.sent-2411-426: Moreover, these preferences change across environments.
elements.sent-2411-427: We assume a finite number of latent environments exist, and at each time step, one of these environments is active.
elements.sent-2411-428: Given that agents have learned their latent vectors, the rankings of arms they form based on mean rewards, observed after processing the feature vectors, would uniquely correspond to one of the latent environments.
elements.sent-2411-429: While small feature perturbations can occur within a particular environment, feature vectors from different environments would lead to distinct rankings.
elements.sent-2411-430: Motivating example: Let us look at the application of Amazon Mechanical Turk.
elements.sent-2411-431: Based on external factors, the (monetary) reward of different tasks may vary with time.
elements.sent-2411-432: For example, before a major product release, companies may pay more for tasks like data cleaning, labeling, processing, rating search result accuracies, identifying issues, and removing redundancies.
elements.sent-2411-433: On the other hand, jobs like filling out surveys or answering questions on various topics have a (more or less) steady pay regardless.
elements.sent-2411-434: Since the worker's goal is to earn maximum reward, it is in the worker's best interest to identify the temporal variability and change their preference accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-435: This contextual framework enables richer and more realistic modeling of markets.
elements.sent-2411-436: It is partly inspired by latent bandit models~\cite{hong_latent_bandits,gopalan2016low , which combine offline learning of complex models from historical interactions with online decision-making, where agents select actions after identifying the latent state.}
elements.sent-2411-437: Apart from bandits literature, latent variable estimation is a classical problem in statistics with applications in clustering and mixture models.
elements.sent-2411-438: Contextual Matching Market: We address a decentralized setup where agents make independent decisions without a central coordinator.
elements.sent-2411-439: A central challenge in such multi-agent systems is resolving collisions, which occur when multiple agents attempt to match with the same arm.
elements.sent-2411-440: Each arm maintains a preference ranking over agents, and in the event of a collision, the arm selects its most preferred agent, granting them a stochastic reward.
elements.sent-2411-441: The remaining agents are informed of their rejection and receive zero reward.
elements.sent-2411-442: This decentralized structure prevents an agent from independently running a standard linear bandit algorithm, such as OFUL \cite{OFUL , as the regret incurred due to collisions is not characterizable.}
elements.sent-2411-443: Algorithm: We propose a novel algorithm, Environment Triggered Phased Gale Shapley (\alg \!) which employs round robin exploration, eliminating collisions.\ \alg leverages least squares estimates to approximate the latent preference vector $\thetai$, which governs the ranking process.
elements.sent-2411-444: Using these estimates, we derive concentration bounds that quantify the deviation between the estimated mean rewards and their actual values.
elements.sent-2411-445: As exploration progresses, the confidence intervals around the estimated rewards narrow, leading to increasingly precise estimates.
elements.sent-2411-446: Once the confidence intervals for the top $N$ arms no longer overlap, agents can confidently identify the correct ranking for different environments.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{ETPGS: Environment-Triggered Phased Gale-Shapley (at agent $i$)} \label{alg:alg1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Explore phase length: $Block(l) := 2^l$ \State Blackboard variable: $\mathcal{B}$ \State Initialize: $D[e{:}(s_i, \sigma_i)] = \{\}$, $\tau_{\text{end}} = 0$, $l = 0$ \For{$t \geq 1$} \State Reset: $\mathcal{B} \gets 1$ \Comment{Environment Recovery} \State $\sigma_i(t) \gets \begin{cases} \sigma[1{:}N], \text{if } \sigma \text{ satisfies Eq.~\eqref{eq:separation}} \emptyset, \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ \If{$\sigma_i(t) \neq \emptyset$} \If{$\sigma_i(t) \notin \{\sigma_i[e] \mid e \in D\}$} \State Add new environment: $D[e] \gets (1, \sigma_i(t))$ \EndIf \State $e_i(t) \gets e$ such that $\sigma_i[e] = \sigma_i(t)$ \Else \State $\mathcal{B} \gets \mathcal{B} \land 0$ \EndIf \Comment{Exploration Triggering} \If{$\mathcal{B} = 0$ and $t > \tau_{\text{end}}$} \State Restart explore: $\tau_{\text{end}} \gets t + Block(l)$, $l \gets l + 1$ \EndIf \Comment{Explore or Gale-Shapley Matching} \If{$t \leq \tau_{\text{end}}$} \Comment{Exploration Phase} \State Play arm: $m_i(t) \gets ((i + t) \bmod K) + 1$ \State Observe reward $r_i(t)$ \State Update estimates: $\hat{\theta}(t), \mathrm{UCB}(t), \mathrm{LCB}(t)$ \Else \Comment{Exploitation Phase} \State Retrieve $(s, \sigma) = D[e_i(t)]$ \State Play GS index: $m_i(t) \gets \sigma[s]$ \If{$m_i(t) = \phi$} \State Update environment pointer: $D[e_i(t)] \gets (s + 1, \sigma)$ \EndIf \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-570: Prolonged exploration to shrink confidence widths for distinguishing the smallest gap, $\Delta_{\min}$, can be expensive, as $\Delta_{\min}$ may approach zero.
elements.sent-2411-571: This section investigates how leveraging the problem's inherent structure can significantly reduce exploration time.
elements.sent-2411-572: Specifically, we exploit the existence of finite reward gap periods instead of assuming the existence of a constant uniform minimum reward gap.
elements.sent-2411-573: The central idea is that if a gap of $\Delta (> \Delta_{\min )$ can be resolved, agents can recover rankings across the environments if this gap $\Delta$ has shown up at least once in each of the environments.}
elements.sent-2411-574: Once rankings are established, the next challenge is identifying the current environment.
elements.sent-2411-575: Even if agents cannot fully resolve the rankings of the top arms, they can still attempt to infer the environment using partial rankings.
elements.sent-2411-576: We enhance the Environment Recovery component of \alg, introducing our improved algorithm: Improved Environment-Triggered Phased Explore and Gale-Shapley (IETP-GS).
